summary:
The Battle of Ctesiphon/Edessa (260 AD) — actually a series of engagements culminating near Edessa in modern Turkey — produced the most humiliating defeat in Roman imperial history: Emperor Valerian was captured alive by the [[event:2059|Sassanid]] king Shapur I, the first and only time a Roman emperor was taken prisoner in battle. The disaster occurred during the [[event:2025|Crisis of the Third Century]], when Valerian led a large army east to counter Sassanid raids into Syria. His army was weakened by the [[event:2088|Plague of Cyprian]], and in the engagement near Edessa, the Romans were surrounded and Valerian was taken captive — an event Shapur immortalized in rock-cut reliefs showing the emperor kneeling or standing before the triumphant Sassanid king.

Valerian's fate in captivity is uncertain but grim according to most ancient sources — he reportedly died in Persian captivity without ever being ransomed or exchanged. Shapur's inscription at Naqsh-e Rostam lists three Roman emperors he defeated (Gordian III, Philip, and Valerian), presenting himself as the dominant ruler of the world. The disaster triggered a political crisis in the Roman Empire: the western provinces proclaimed their own emperor (the Gallic Empire), the eastern provinces temporarily fell under the control of [[event:2855|Palmyrene Empire]] queen Zenobia, and the empire seemed on the verge of permanent fragmentation. The emperor Aurelian eventually restored Roman unity ([[event:2854|Aurelian Restores Empire]]), but Valerian's capture remained a potent symbol of Roman vulnerability against the Sassanid rival.

read_more_links.title: Shapur I rock relief Naqsh-e Rostam
